text: Identifies the behavioral axes to measure — at minimum triggering (does it auto-activate on the right prompts = recall, and stay quiet on near-misses = specificity) and whether the output satisfies the skill's own discipline (correct-usage); ideally also whether it beats the no-skill baseline.
Proposes a trigger dataset containing positive prompts AND near-miss negative prompts (not positives only), so recall and false-fire specificity are both tested.
Proposes scoring the output against an explicit rubric of observable, weighted criteria rather than eyeballing quality.

Compares the skill against a skill-free baseline (with vs without), and shows awareness that the baseline must be genuinely isolated/skill-free rather than the same environment with the skill still loaded.
Notes that this means many headless runs (cost/time) and that repeats plus confidence intervals are needed because activation is non-deterministic — not a single-run conclusion.
Frames the exercise as measuring behavior (does it fire and help), distinct from a qualitative design review of whether the skill is well-built.
